sql: SELECT p.Id, p.Title FROM posts p INNER JOIN comments c ON p.Id = c.PostId GROUP BY p.Id ORDER BY COUNT(c.Id) DESC LIMIT 10;
hidden_knowledge: By popular I mean posts with the highest number of associated comments.

question: I'm analyzing community engagement on our platform and want to highlight examples of high-quality interactions. Could you retrieve all comments that have a high score to use as case studies in our best practices guide?
sql: SELECT Id, PostId, Text, Score FROM comments WHERE Score >= 10;
hidden_knowledge: By high score, I mean comments with a score of 10 or more points based on community upvotes.

question: I'm analyzing user engagement on a platform and want to understand which comments are considered valuable by the community. Could you retrieve all valuable comments along with their authors and associated post titles? This will help us identify content that resonates well with users.
sql:
SELECT c.Id AS comment_id, c.Text AS comment_text, u.DisplayName AS author_name, p.Title AS post_title
FROM comments c
INNER JOIN users u ON c.UserId = u.Id
INNER JOIN posts p ON c.PostId = p.Id
WHERE c.Score > (SELECT AVG(Score) FROM comments);
hidden_knowledge: By valuable, I mean comments that have scores above the average score of all comments on the platform.

question: I'm analyzing user engagement on a platform and want to understand which comments are considered valuable by the community. Could you retrieve all valuable comments along with their authors and associated post titles? This will help us identify content that resonates well with users.
sql:
SELECT c.Id AS comment_id, c.Text AS comment_text, u.DisplayName AS author_name, p.Title AS post_title
FROM comments c
INNER JOIN users u ON c.UserId = u.Id
INNER JOIN posts p ON c.PostId = p.Id
WHERE c.Score >= 10;
hidden_knowledge: By valuable, I mean comments that have received at least 10 upvotes based on community standards.

question: I'm analyzing user engagement on a Q&A platform and need to identify popular questions for inclusion in a monthly highlights report. Can you retrieve all popular questions from the database?
sql:
WITH EngagementRank AS ( 
    SELECT *, 
           NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY Score DESC) AS score_decile
    FROM posts
)
SELECT *
FROM EngagementRank
WHERE PostTypeId = 1 AND score_decile = 1;
hidden_knowledge: By 'popular', I mean questions that are in the top 10% of all questions ranked by their vote scores.

question: I'm analyzing user engagement on a Q&A platform and need to identify popular questions for inclusion in a monthly highlights report. Can you retrieve all popular questions from the database?
sql:
WITH EngagementRank AS (
    SELECT *,
           NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY CommentCount DESC) AS comment_decile
    FROM posts
)
SELECT *
FROM EngagementRank
WHERE PostTypeId = 1 AND comment_decile = 1;
hidden_knowledge: By 'popular', I mean questions that are in the top 10% of all questions ranked by number of comments received.

question: List popular posts.
sql: SELECT * FROM posts WHERE ViewCount > (SELECT AVG(ViewCount) FROM posts);
hidden_knowledge: By popular, I mean posts with a view count higher than the average view count across all posts.

question: List popular posts.
sql: WITH PostRank AS (SELECT Id, Score, PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Score DESC) AS pr FROM posts) SELECT Id FROM PostRank WHERE pr < 0.2;
hidden_knowledge: By popular posts I mean those in the top 20% based on their scores.

question: Identify all players with high potential scores based on their attribute records.
sql: SELECT player_api_id FROM Player_Attributes WHERE potential > 85;
hidden_knowledge: By high potential scores I mean values exceeding industry benchmarks above 85.

question: Identify all players who have high overall ratings based on their attributes.
sql: SELECT p.player_name, pa.overall_rating FROM Player p INNER JOIN Player_Attributes pa ON p.player_api_id = pa.player_api_id WHERE pa.overall_rating > 90;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high overall ratings', I mean players whose rating exceeds an absolute threshold of 90.

question: Identify all players who demonstrate high overall performance based on their ratings.
sql: WITH RatingPercentiles AS (SELECT player_api_id, overall_rating, NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY overall_rating DESC) AS rating_quintile FROM Player_Attributes), TopQuintile AS (SELECT player_api_id FROM RatingPercentiles WHERE rating_quintile = 1) SELECT p.player_name, pa.overall_rating FROM Player p JOIN Player_Attributes pa ON p.player_api_id = pa.player_api_id WHERE pa.player_api_id IN (SELECT player_api_id FROM TopQuintile);
hidden_knowledge: By high performance, I mean players in the top 20% of overall ratings when ranked in descending order.

question: Hey, can you find all the recent player evaluations so I can see how they've been performing lately?
sql: SELECT * FROM Player_Attributes WHERE date >= '2015-02-01'
hidden_knowledge: By recent evaluations, I mean any records from the last year up to February 2016.

question: Hey! Can you list all players with high ball control? I want to know who stands out in that area.
sql:
SELECT p.player_name 
FROM Player p
JOIN Player_Attributes pa 
ON p.player_api_id = pa.player_api_id
WHERE pa.ball_control > 85;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high ball control', I mean any historical record where their ball control rating exceeded 85.

question: Hey! Can you list all players with high ball control? I want to know who stands out in that area.
sql:
SELECT p.player_name 
FROM Player p
JOIN (
    SELECT player_api_id 
    FROM Player_Attributes
    WHERE (player_api_id, date) IN (
        SELECT player_api_id, MAX(date)
        FROM Player_Attributes
        GROUP BY player_api_id
    )
    AND ball_control > 90
) AS recent_pa 
ON p.player_api_id = recent_pa.player_api_id;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high ball control', I specifically mean players whose most recent recorded ball control score is above 90.

question: Show players with high overall ratings.
sql: SELECT * FROM Player_Attributes WHERE overall_rating > 80;
hidden_knowledge: By high, I mean players whose overall rating exceeds 80.

question: Show me all players with high overall ratings.
sql: WITH LatestAttributes AS (SELECT player_api_id, overall_rating, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY player_api_id ORDER BY date DESC) AS rn FROM Player_Attributes), DistinctPlayers AS (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY overall_rating DESC) AS rnk, COUNT(*) OVER () AS total_players FROM LatestAttributes WHERE rn = 1) SELECT p.player_name FROM DistinctPlayers dp JOIN Player p ON dp.player_api_id = p.player_api_id WHERE dp.rnk <= dp.total_players * 0.1;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high', I mean the top 10% of players by their most recent overall rating.

question: Which players have high potential according to their attributes?
sql: SELECT p.player_name, pa.potential FROM Player p INNER JOIN Player_Attributes pa ON p.player_api_id = pa.player_api_id WHERE pa.potential > 80;
hidden_knowledge: By high potential, I mean players with a potential score strictly above 80.

question: Which players have high potential according to their attributes?
sql: SELECT p.player_name, pa.potential FROM Player p INNER JOIN Player_Attributes pa ON p.player_api_id = pa.player_api_id WHERE pa.potential > (SELECT AVG(potential) FROM Player_Attributes);
hidden_knowledge: By high potential, I mean players whose potential exceeds the average potential score of all recorded players.

question: I'm analyzing player development trends for our sports academy's scouting report. We need to identify players who currently have high potential based on their attributes to prioritize recruitment efforts.
sql:
SELECT p.player_name, pa.potential 
FROM Player p 
JOIN Player_Attributes pa ON p.player_api_id = pa.player_api_id 
WHERE pa.potential > 85;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high potential,' I mean players whose raw potential score exceeds an arbitrarily defined threshold of 85 based on our scouting standards.

question: List matches with high home team goals.
sql: SELECT * FROM Match WHERE home_team_goal >= 3;
hidden_knowledge: By high home team goals I mean three or more goals scored by the home team.

question: List players with high potential.
sql: WITH ranked_potential AS (SELECT player_api_id, potential, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY potential DESC) AS rnk FROM Player_Attributes), total_players AS (SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM Player_Attributes) SELECT DISTINCT p.player_name FROM Player p INNER JOIN Player_Attributes pa ON p.player_api_id = pa.player_api_id WHERE pa.potential >= (SELECT potential FROM ranked_potential CROSS JOIN total_players WHERE rnk = CEIL(cnt * 0.1));
hidden_knowledge: By high potential, I mean players whose potential ranks within the top 10% of all recorded player potentials.

question: Find players who frequently participate in matches.
sql:
WITH AllPlayersCTE AS (
    SELECT home_player_1 AS player_api_id FROM Match WHERE home_player_1 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT home_player_2 FROM Match WHERE home_player_2 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT home_player_3 FROM Match WHERE home_player_3 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT home_player_4 FROM Match WHERE home_player_4 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT home_player_5 FROM Match WHERE home_player_5 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT home_player_6 FROM Match WHERE home_player_6 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT home_player_7 FROM Match WHERE home_player_7 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT home_player_8 FROM Match WHERE home_player_8 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT home_player_9 FROM Match WHERE home_player_9 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT home_player_10 FROM Match WHERE home_player_10 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT home_player_11 FROM Match WHERE home_player_11 IS NOT NULL

    UNION ALL

    SELECT away_player_1 AS player_api_id FROM Match WHERE away_player_1 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT away_player_2 FROM Match WHERE away_player_2 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT away_player_3 FROM Match WHERE away_player_3 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT away_player_4 FROM Match WHERE away_player_4 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT away_player_5 FROM Match WHERE away_player_5 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT away_player_6 FROM Match WHERE away_player_6 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT away_player_7 FROM Match WHERE away_player_7 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT away_player_8 FROM Match WHERE away_player_8 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT away_player_9 FROM Match WHERE away_player_9 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT away_player_10 FROM Match WHERE away_player_10 IS NOT NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT away_player_11 FROM Match WHERE away_player_11 IS NOT NULL)
SELECT p.player_api_id, p.player_name, COUNT(*) AS total_matches 
FROM AllPlayersCTE ap 
JOIN Player p ON ap.player_api_id = p.player_api_id 
GROUP BY p.player_api_id 
HAVING COUNT(*) > 30;
hidden_knowledge: By 'frequently participate', I mean players who appear in over 30 matches.

question: Identify top-performing constructors based on their performance metrics in recent standings events.
sql: SELECT * FROM constructorStandings ORDER BY wins DESC LIMIT 5;
hidden_knowledge: By top-performing constructors, I mean those with the most race wins recorded in standing records.

question: Identify all constructors associated with high performance in the 2009 Formula One season.
sql:
SELECT c.name, SUM(cr.points) AS total_points
FROM constructors c
JOIN constructorResults cr ON c.constructorId = cr.constructorId
JOIN races r ON cr.raceId = r.raceId
WHERE r.year = 2009
GROUP BY c.constructorId
HAVING total_points > (
    SELECT AVG(total_points)
    FROM (
        SELECT SUM(points) AS total_points
        FROM constructorResults
        JOIN races ON constructorResults.raceId = races.raceId
        WHERE races.year = 2009
        GROUP BY constructorResults.constructorId
    )
);
hidden_knowledge: By 'high performance', I mean any constructor whose total points in the 2009 season exceeded the average performance of all teams during that year.

question: Identify all constructors who achieved success in the most recent Formula One season.
sql: WITH LatestSeason AS (SELECT year FROM seasons ORDER BY year DESC LIMIT 1), SeasonRaces AS (SELECT raceId FROM races WHERE year = (SELECT year FROM LatestSeason)), ConstructorPoints AS (SELECT c.constructorId, SUM(cr.points) AS totalPoints FROM constructorResults cr JOIN SeasonRaces sr ON cr.raceId = sr.raceId JOIN constructors c ON cr.constructorId = c.constructorId GROUP BY c.constructorId) SELECT c.name FROM constructors c JOIN ConstructorPoints cp ON c.constructorId = cp.constructorId WHERE cp.totalPoints IN (SELECT totalPoints FROM ConstructorPoints ORDER BY totalPoints DESC LIMIT 5);
hidden_knowledge: By success, I mean constructors who accumulated among the top five highest total points across all races in the most recent Formula One season.

question: Identify high-performing constructors in the most recent Formula One season.
sql: WITH LatestSeason AS (SELECT MAX(year) AS year FROM races), ConstructorAverages AS (SELECT cr.constructorId, AVG(cr.points) AS avg_points, COUNT(*) AS total_races FROM constructorResults cr JOIN races r ON cr.raceId = r.raceId WHERE r.year = (SELECT year FROM LatestSeason) GROUP BY cr.constructorId HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5), FilteredConstructors AS (SELECT constructorId FROM ConstructorAverages WHERE avg_points >= 20) SELECT c.name, ca.avg_points, ca.total_races FROM FilteredConstructors fc JOIN constructors c ON fc.constructorId = c.constructorId JOIN ConstructorAverages ca ON fc.constructorId = ca.constructorId ORDER BY ca.avg_points DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By high-performing, I mean constructors who achieved an average of at least 20 points per race in the most recent season and participated in at least five races.

question: Identify high-performing constructors in the most recent Formula One season.
sql: WITH LatestSeason AS (SELECT MAX(year) AS year FROM races), RaceParticipation AS (SELECT cs.constructorId, COUNT(*) AS total_races_in_season FROM constructorStandings cs JOIN races r ON cs.raceId = r.raceId WHERE r.year = (SELECT year FROM LatestSeason) GROUP BY cs.constructorId HAVING COUNT(*) >= 10), PodiumAchievements AS (SELECT cs.constructorId, COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE position <= 3) AS podium_count FROM constructorStandings cs JOIN races r ON cs.raceId = r.raceId WHERE r.year = (SELECT year FROM LatestSeason) GROUP BY cs.constructorId HAVING COUNT(*) >= 10), QualifiedConstructors AS (SELECT pa.constructorId, pa.podium_count, rp.total_races_in_season FROM PodiumAchievements pa JOIN RaceParticipation rp ON pa.constructorId = rp.constructorId WHERE pa.podium_count >= FLOOR(rp.total_races_in_season * 0.5)) SELECT c.name, qc.podium_count, qc.total_races_in_season FROM QualifiedConstructors qc JOIN constructors c ON qc.constructorId = c.constructorId ORDER BY qc.podium_count DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By high-performing, I mean constructors who achieved podium positions (positions 1-3) in at least half of the races in which they participated during the most recent season.

question: Hey! Could you show me the most successful Formula 1 teams? I want to see their names and how well they did.
sql:
SELECT c.name AS team_name, SUM(cs.points) AS total_championship_points
FROM constructors c
INNER JOIN constructorStandings cs ON c.constructorId = cs.constructorId
GROUP BY c.constructorId
ORDER BY total_championship_points DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'successful', I mean teams with the highest cumulative championship points across all seasons.

question: Hey! Can you find all the circuits that are considered popular for hosting races in Formula One? I'm trying to figure out which tracks get chosen most often.
sql:
-- Interpreting 'popular' as top 5 circuits by total number of races hosted since 2000
WITH RaceFrequency AS (
    SELECT 
        c.circuitId,
        c.name,
        COUNT(r.raceId) AS totalRaces,
        RANK() OVER(ORDER BY COUNT(r.raceId) DESC) AS popularityRank
    FROM circuits c
    JOIN races r ON c.circuitId = r.circuitId
    WHERE r.year >= 2000
    GROUP BY c.circuitId
)
SELECT name, totalRaces
FROM RaceFrequency
WHERE popularityRank <= 5;
hidden_knowledge: "By 'popular' I mean the circuits that have hosted the most actual races since the year 2000."

question: Hey! Can you find all the circuits that are considered popular for hosting races in Formula One? I'm trying to figure out which tracks get chosen most often.
sql:
-- Interpreting 'popular' as circuits with most distinct years of hosting since Formula One inception
WITH YearlyUsage AS (
    SELECT 
        c.circuitId,
        c.name,
        COUNT(DISTINCT r.year) AS distinctYearsHosted,
        NTILE(4) OVER(ORDER BY COUNT(DISTINCT r.year) DESC) AS usageTier
    FROM circuits c
    JOIN races r ON c.circuitId = r.circuitId
    GROUP BY c.circuitId
)
SELECT name, distinctYearsHosted
FROM YearlyUsage
WHERE usageTier = 1;
hidden_knowledge: "When I said 'popular', I actually meant the tracks that have been regularly used across different years in the Formula One calendar."

question: List young drivers.
sql: SELECT * FROM drivers WHERE strftime('%Y', dob) > '1995';
hidden_knowledge: By 'young,' I mean drivers born after 1995.

question: Show me constructors with many race wins.
sql: SELECT c.constructorId, c.name, COUNT(*) AS total_wins FROM constructors c INNER JOIN results r ON c.constructorId = r.constructorId WHERE r.positionOrder = 1 GROUP BY c.constructorId HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many race wins', I mean constructors who have won more than five races in their history.

question: Show me constructors with many race wins.
sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT c.constructorId, c.name, COUNT(*) AS total_wins FROM constructors c INNER JOIN results r ON c.constructorId = r.constructorId WHERE r.positionOrder = 1 GROUP BY c.constructorId ORDER BY total_wins DESC LIMIT (SELECT CEIL(COUNT(*) * 0.1) FROM constructors))
hidden_knowledge: By 'many race wins', I mean the top ten percent of constructors based on their total number of race victories.

question: Show successful Formula 1 constructors in recent seasons.
sql: WITH LatestSeason AS (SELECT MAX(year) AS year FROM races) SELECT c.name, cs.points FROM constructors c JOIN constructorStandings cs ON c.constructorId = cs.constructorId JOIN races r ON cs.raceId = r.raceId JOIN LatestSeason ls ON r.year = ls.year WHERE cs.position <= 3 ORDER BY cs.points DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'successful', I mean constructors who finished in the top 3 positions based on championship points in the most recent season.

question: Which constructors had high points in the 2009 season?
sql:
SELECT c.name AS constructor_name 
FROM constructors c 
JOIN constructorResults cr ON c.constructorId = cr.constructorId 
JOIN races r ON cr.raceId = r.raceId 
WHERE r.year = 2009 
GROUP BY c.constructorId 
HAVING SUM(cr.points) > ( 
    SELECT AVG(total_points) 
    FROM (
        SELECT SUM(cr.points) AS total_points 
        FROM constructorResults cr 
        JOIN races r ON cr.raceId = r.raceId 
        WHERE r.year = 2009 
        GROUP BY cr.constructorId
    ) AS season_averages
);
hidden_knowledge: By high points, I mean constructors whose cumulative points exceeded the average of all participating constructors during the 2009 Formula One World Championship.

question: Which constructors had high points in the 2009 season?
sql:
SELECT c.name AS constructor_name 
FROM constructors c 
JOIN (
    SELECT constructorId 
    FROM (
        SELECT cr.constructorId, SUM(cr.points) AS total_points,
               RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(cr.points) DESC) AS rank
        FROM constructorResults cr
        JOIN races r ON cr.raceId = r.raceId
        WHERE r.year = 2009
        GROUP BY cr.constructorId
    ) ranked
    WHERE rank <= 2
) ranked_constructors ON c.constructorId = ranked_constructors.constructorId;
hidden_knowledge: By high points, I mean only those constructors who secured positions within the top two rankings based on their cumulative point totals from all races in the 2009 season.

question: Which constructors were successful in the most recent season?
sql: WITH LatestSeason AS (SELECT MAX(year) AS latest_year FROM races) SELECT c.constructorId, c.name, SUM(cs.points) AS total_points FROM constructorStandings cs JOIN constructors c ON cs.constructorId = c.constructorId JOIN races r ON cs.raceId = r.raceId JOIN LatestSeason ls ON r.year = ls.latest_year GROUP BY c.constructorId HAVING total_points > (SELECT AVG(points) FROM constructorStandings cs2 JOIN races r2 ON cs2.raceId = r2.raceId WHERE r2.year = ls.latest_year) ORDER BY total_points DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By successful, I mean constructors whose total points in that season exceeded the average points across all teams in that season.

question: I'm preparing an analysis on Formula One team performance for a motorsport publication. I need to identify successful constructors who have demonstrated strong performance in recent seasons.
sql: SELECT constructorId, position, points, wins FROM constructorStandings WHERE position <= 3 ORDER BY position ASC;
hidden_knowledge: By successful, I mean constructors who have finished in top positions (1st to 3rd place) in recent standings.

question: I'm analyzing historical data for a Formula One tourism initiative aimed at promoting fan experiences at iconic tracks. To prioritize locations for our exclusive event packages, we need to identify circuits that are considered popular based on their frequency of hosting Grand Prix events over the years.
sql: SELECT c.name AS circuit_name, COUNT(r.raceId) AS num_hosted_events, MAX(r.year) AS last_hosted_year FROM circuits c INNER JOIN races r ON c.circuitId = r.circuitId GROUP BY c.circuitId ORDER BY num_hosted_events DESC LIMIT 5;
hidden_knowledge: By popular circuits based on hosting frequency, I mean those that have hosted Grand Prix events most frequently compared to others—specifically the top five most frequently used tracks.

question: I'm analyzing historical data for a Formula One tourism initiative aimed at promoting fan experiences at iconic tracks. To prioritize locations for our exclusive event packages, we need to identify circuits that are considered popular based on their frequency of hosting Grand Prix events over the years.
sql: SELECT c.name AS circuit_name, COUNT(r.raceId) AS num_hosted_events, MAX(r.year) AS last_hosted_year FROM circuits c INNER JOIN races r ON c.circuitId = r.circuitId GROUP BY c.circuitId HAVING COUNT(r.raceId) > 20;
hidden_knowledge: By popular circuits based on hosting frequency, I mean those that have hosted more than twenty Grand Prix events throughout history.

question: Show top constructors based on performance.
sql: SELECT c.name, SUM(cr.points) AS total_points FROM constructors c INNER JOIN constructorResults cr ON c.constructorId = cr.constructorId GROUP BY c.constructorId ORDER BY total_points DESC LIMIT 10;
hidden_knowledge: By top constructors, I mean those with the highest cumulative points across all seasons in Formula One history.

question: Show top constructors based on performance.
sql: SELECT c.name, SUM(cr.points) AS recent_points FROM constructors c INNER JOIN constructorResults cr ON c.constructorId = cr.constructorId INNER JOIN races r ON cr.raceId = r.raceId WHERE r.year = (SELECT MAX(year) FROM races) GROUP BY c.constructorId ORDER BY recent_points DESC LIMIT 10;
hidden_knowledge: By top constructors, I mean those with the highest points total specifically in the most recent completed Formula One season.

question: Show popular circuits.
sql: WITH CircuitRaceCounts AS (SELECT c.circuitId, c.name, COUNT(r.raceId) AS total_races FROM circuits c JOIN races r ON c.circuitId = r.circuitId GROUP BY c.circuitId), FilteredCircuits AS (SELECT * FROM CircuitRaceCounts WHERE total_races > (SELECT AVG(total_races) FROM CircuitRaceCounts)) SELECT name FROM FilteredCircuits ORDER BY total_races DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By popular circuits I mean those that host more races than the average number of races across all circuits.

question: Which charter schools are doing really well in terms of SAT scores? Show me the ones that are performing above average.
sql: SELECT sname, AvgScrRead, AvgScrMath, AvgScrWrite FROM satscores INNER JOIN schools ON satscores.cds = schools.CDSCode WHERE Charter = 1 AND ((AvgScrRead + AvgScrMath + AvgScrWrite) / 3) > 550 ORDER BY (AvgScrRead + AvgScrMath + AvgScrWrite) DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By doing really well, I mean charter schools where the average combined SAT score is above 550.

question: List old school buildings on campus
sql: SELECT School Name, OpenDate FROM schools WHERE OpenDate < '2000-01-01';
hidden_knowledge: By old school buildings, I mean those opened before the year 2000.

question: Which counties have recent SAT score data?
sql: SELECT DISTINCT cname FROM satscores WHERE rtype = 'S' AND sname IS NOT NULL;

question: Which schools have a high percentage of students eligible for free or reduced-price meals?
sql: WITH SchoolFRPMStats AS (    SELECT        'School Name',        'Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12)',        AVG('Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12)') OVER () AS AvgFRPM    FROM frpm ) SELECT 'School Name', 'Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12)' FROM SchoolFRPMStats WHERE 'Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12)' > AvgFRPM ORDER BY 'Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12)' DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high percentage', I mean schools where the percentage of students eligible for free or reduced-price meals is above the average for all schools.

question: Retrieve the details of users who have received a significant number of badges, focusing on those with the most distinguished achievements.
sql: WITH UserBadgeStats AS (SELECT UserId, COUNT(*) AS BadgeCount FROM badges GROUP BY UserId), RankedUsers AS (SELECT UserId, BadgeCount, PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY BadgeCount DESC) AS Percentile FROM UserBadgeStats) SELECT u.Id, u.DisplayName, r.BadgeCount FROM users u JOIN RankedUsers r ON u.Id = r.UserId WHERE r.Percentile <= 0.1;
hidden_knowledge: By 'significant number of badges,' I mean users who are in the top 10% of users based on the number of badges they have received.

question: Hey! Can you list all the posts with many comments? These should be the most engaging ones.
sql: SELECT Id, Title, CommentCount FROM posts WHERE CommentCount > 5 ORDER BY CommentCount DESC;
hidden_knowledge: 'Many comments' means more than 5 comments per post.

question: Hey buddy! Can you find all the posts with many answers? I'm interested in the most discussed topics.
sql: SELECT Id, Title, AnswerCount FROM posts WHERE AnswerCount > (SELECT AVG(AnswerCount) FROM posts) ORDER BY AnswerCount DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many answers,' I mean posts with answers above the average answer count.

question: Hey, can you show me the top users based on their reputation? I'd love to see who's really popular around here.
sql: WITH UserReputation AS ( SELECT Id, DisplayName, Reputation, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Reputation DESC) AS reputation_rank FROM users ) SELECT ur.DisplayName, ur.Reputation FROM UserReputation ur WHERE ur.reputation_rank <= 10;
hidden_knowledge: By 'popular' I mean users with the highest reputation scores.

question: Hey, can you show me the top users based on their reputation? I'd love to see who's really popular around here.
sql: WITH UserActivity AS ( SELECT u.Id, u.DisplayName, COUNT(v.Id) AS vote_count, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(v.Id) DESC) AS activity_rank FROM users u LEFT JOIN votes v ON u.Id = v.UserId GROUP BY u.Id, u.DisplayName ) SELECT ua.DisplayName, ua.vote_count FROM UserActivity ua WHERE ua.activity_rank <= 10;
hidden_knowledge: By 'popular' I mean users who have received the most votes, indicating higher activity.

question: Who are the top users by reputation?
sql: SELECT DisplayName, Reputation FROM users WHERE Reputation > 10000 ORDER BY Reputation DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'top users', I mean users with a reputation greater than 10,000.

question: I'm reviewing our content management strategy and need to evaluate the performance of different types of posts. Could you help me find the posts that have generated a lot of interest from our community members?
sql: SELECT Id, Title, ViewCount FROM posts WHERE ViewCount > 1000 ORDER BY ViewCount DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By a lot of interest, I mean posts with over 1000 views.

question: For a team meeting, I need to gather information about posts that have received a high level of community attention. Could you provide details about posts with substantial view counts and their associated tags?
sql: SELECT p.Title, p.ViewCount, t.TagName FROM posts p LEFT JOIN tags t ON p.Tags LIKE '%' + t.TagName + '%' WHERE p.ViewCount > 1000 ORDER BY p.ViewCount DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'substantial view counts,' I mean posts with over 1000 views.

question: I'm analyzing user engagement patterns on our platform and would like to identify the most active users based on their comment activity. To better understand our community dynamics, please provide a list of users who have made the most comments, along with their comment statistics. This will help us recognize and potentially reward our most vocal contributors.
sql:
WITH UserCommentStats AS (
    SELECT
        u.Id AS UserId,
        u.DisplayName,
        COUNT(c.Id) AS CommentCount,
        AVG(c.Score) AS AverageScore,
        MAX(c.CreationDate) AS LastCommentDate
    FROM
        users u
    INNER JOIN
        comments c ON u.Id = c.UserId
    GROUP BY
        u.Id, u.DisplayName
)
SELECT
    UserId,
    DisplayName,
    CommentCount,
    AverageScore,
    LastCommentDate
FROM
    UserCommentStats
WHERE CommentCount >= (SELECT AVG(CommentCount) FROM UserCommentStats)
ORDER BY CommentCount DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'most active users' I mean all users whose comment count is above the average comment count across all users.

question: Show highly scored posts.
sql: SELECT Id, Title, Score FROM posts WHERE Score > 50 ORDER BY Score DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By highly scored posts, I mean those with a score above 50.

question: Identify constructors with a notable presence in recent Formula One championships, considering both their points accumulation and racing standings.
sql: WITH TopConstructors AS ( SELECT constructorId, COUNT(*) AS championshipWins FROM constructorStandings WHERE positionText = '1' GROUP BY constructorId HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2 ), ConsistentPerformers AS ( SELECT constructorId, AVG(points) AS avgPoints FROM constructorResults GROUP BY constructorId HAVING AVG(points) >= (SELECT AVG(points) * 1.2 FROM constructorResults) ) SELECT c.name, tc.championshipWins, cp.avgPoints FROM constructors c JOIN TopConstructors tc ON c.constructorId = tc.constructorId JOIN ConsistentPerformers cp ON c.constructorId = cp.constructorId ORDER BY tc.championshipWins DESC, cp.avgPoints DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By notable presence in recent championships, I mean constructors who have won at least two championships and consistently performed above the average points accumulation across all races.

question: List constructors with many wins

question: I'm analyzing the performance trends of Formula 1 constructors over time for a comprehensive industry report. I need to identify constructors that have consistently performed well but might be experiencing a decline in their competitive edge. Specifically, I want to see constructors with high points totals in recent seasons but who have shown a downward trend in their performance.
sql:
WITH constructor_points AS (
    SELECT
        c.constructorId,
        c.name,
        s.year,
        SUM(rs.points) AS total_points
    FROM
        constructorResults rs
    JOIN
        races r ON rs.raceId = r.raceId
    JOIN
        seasons s ON r.year = s.year
    JOIN
        constructors c ON rs.constructorId = c.constructorId
    WHERE
        s.year BETWEEN (SELECT MAX(year) FROM seasons) - 5 AND (SELECT MAX(year) FROM seasons)
    GROUP BY
        c.constructorId, c.name, s.year
),
constructor_trends AS (
    SELECT
        constructorId,
        name,
        AVG(total_points) AS avg_points,
        (SELECT total_points FROM constructor_points cp2 WHERE cp2.constructorId = cp1.constructorId AND cp2.year = (SELECT MAX(year) FROM seasons)) AS latest_year_points,
        (SELECT total_points FROM constructor_points cp3 WHERE cp3.constructorId = cp1.constructorId AND cp3.year = (SELECT MAX(year) FROM seasons) - 1) AS previous_year_points
    FROM
        constructor_points cp1
    GROUP BY
        constructorId, name
)
SELECT *
FROM constructor_trends
WHERE avg_points > 50 -- Assuming 'high' means average points over 50 per year
  AND latest_year_points < previous_year_points -- Downward trend in latest year compared to previous year
hidden_knowledge: By 'high' points totals, I mean an average of more than 50 points per year over the last five seasons, and by 'recent' I'm referring to the most recent season compared to the previous one.

question: I'm analyzing the performance trends of Formula 1 constructors over time for a comprehensive industry report. I need to identify constructors that have consistently performed well but might be experiencing a decline in their competitive edge. Specifically, I want to see constructors with high points totals in recent seasons but who have shown a downward trend in their performance.
sql:
WITH constructor_season_stats AS (
    SELECT
        c.constructorId,
        c.name,
        s.year,
        SUM(rs.points) AS total_points,
        RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY s.year ORDER BY SUM(rs.points) DESC) AS season_rank
    FROM
        constructorResults rs
    JOIN
        races r ON rs.raceId = r.raceId
    JOIN
        seasons s ON r.year = s.year
    JOIN
        constructors c ON rs.constructorId = c.constructorId
    WHERE
        s.year BETWEEN (SELECT MAX(year) FROM seasons) - 3 AND (SELECT MAX(year) FROM seasons)
    GROUP BY
        c.constructorId, c.name, s.year
),
constructor_performance AS (
    SELECT
        constructorId,
        name,
        AVG(total_points) AS avg_points,
        MIN(season_rank) AS best_rank,
        AVG(season_rank) AS avg_rank,
        (SELECT total_points FROM constructor_season_stats css2 WHERE css2.constructorId = css1.constructorId AND css2.year = (SELECT MAX(year) FROM seasons)) AS latest_year_points,
        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM constructor_season_stats css3 WHERE css3.constructorId = css1.constructorId AND css3.season_rank <= 5 AND css3.year BETWEEN (SELECT MAX(year) FROM seasons) - 3 AND (SELECT MAX(year) FROM seasons)) AS top_five_count
    FROM
        constructor_season_stats css1
    GROUP BY
        constructorId, name
)
SELECT *
FROM constructor_performance
WHERE avg_points > 60 -- Higher threshold for 'high'
  AND best_rank <= 3 -- Consistently top performers with at least one top 3 finish in the last 3 years
  AND top_five_count >= 2 -- Appeared in top 5 at least twice in last 3 years
hidden_knowledge: By 'high' points totals, I mean an average of more than 60 points per year over the last three seasons, and by 'consistently performed well' I mean having at least one top 3 finish and appearing in the top 5 at least twice in those three years.

question: Identify all superheroes with high intelligence scores and their corresponding publishers.
sql:
WITH AvgIntel AS (
    SELECT AVG(attribute_value) AS avg_intel
    FROM hero_attribute
    WHERE attribute_id = (
        SELECT id FROM attribute 
        WHERE attribute_name = 'Intelligence'
    )
)
SELECT 
    s.superhero_name,
    p.publisher_name
FROM superhero s
JOIN hero_attribute ha ON s.id = ha.hero_id
JOIN publisher p ON s.publisher_id = p.id
WHERE ha.attribute_id = (
        SELECT id FROM attribute 
        WHERE attribute_name = 'Intelligence'
    )
AND ha.attribute_value > (SELECT avg_intel FROM AvgIntel);
hidden_knowledge: By high intelligence scores, I mean any score exceeding the average intelligence score across all superheroes.

question: Identify all superheroes with high intelligence scores and their corresponding publishers.
sql:
WITH RankIntel AS (
    SELECT 
        ha.hero_id,
        NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY ha.attribute_value DESC) AS quartile
    FROM hero_attribute ha
    JOIN superhero s ON ha.hero_id = s.id
    WHERE ha.attribute_id = (
        SELECT id FROM attribute 
        WHERE attribute_name = 'Intelligence'
    )
)
SELECT 
    s.superhero_name,
    p.publisher_name
FROM RankIntel ri
JOIN superhero s ON ri.hero_id = s.id
JOIN hero_attribute ha ON ri.hero_id = ha.hero_id AND ha.attribute_id = (
        SELECT id FROM attribute 
        WHERE attribute_name = 'Intelligence'
    )
JOIN publisher p ON s.publisher_id = p.id
WHERE ri.quartile = 1;
hidden_knowledge: By high intelligence scores, I mean those falling within the top quartile of all recorded intelligence scores.

question: Show superheroes with high intelligence.

WITH IntelligenceCTE AS (
    SELECT ha.hero_id,
           ha.attribute_value,
           NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY ha.attribute_value DESC) AS ntile_rank
    FROM hero_attribute ha
    JOIN attribute a ON ha.attribute_id = a.id
    WHERE a.attribute_name = 'Intelligence'
)
SELECT s.superhero_name
FROM IntelligenceCTE cte
JOIN superhero s ON cte.hero_id = s.id
WHERE cte.ntile_rank = 1;

question: Show powerful superheroes.
sql:
WITH SingleAttributeDominance AS (
    SELECT 
        ha.hero_id,
        MAX(ha.attribute_value) AS peak_power,
        PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY MAX(ha.attribute_value) DESC) AS dominance_rank
    FROM hero_attribute ha
    GROUP BY ha.hero_id
),
EliteSuperheroes AS (
    SELECT 
        s.id,
        s.superhero_name,
        sad.peak_power,
        CASE WHEN sad.dominance_rank < 0.1 THEN 'Top 10%' ELSE 'Other' END AS power_bracket
    FROM SingleAttributeDominance sad
    JOIN superhero s ON sad.hero_id = s.id
)
SELECT 
    superhero_name,
    peak_power,
    COUNT(*) OVER () || ' in Top 10%' bracket_context
FROM EliteSuperheroes
WHERE power_bracket = 'Top 10%'
ORDER BY peak_power DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By powerful superheroes, I mean those exhibiting exceptional dominance in at least one specific area - their highest attribute score should place them in the top decile of all recorded attribute values.

question: Identify molecules with many atoms.
sql:
SELECT m.molecule_id
FROM molecule m
JOIN atom a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id
GROUP BY m.molecule_id
HAVING COUNT(a.atom_id) > 5;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many atoms', I mean molecules containing more than five atomic constituents.

question: Identify all molecules that have many bonds.
sql:
WITH BondCounts AS (
    SELECT molecule_id, COUNT(bond_id) AS total_bonds 
    FROM bond 
    GROUP BY molecule_id
), AverageBonds AS (
    SELECT AVG(total_bonds) AS avg_bonds 
    FROM BondCounts
)
SELECT bc.molecule_id 
FROM BondCounts bc, AverageBonds ab 
WHERE bc.total_bonds > ab.avg_bonds;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many bonds', I mean molecules whose total number of bonds exceeds the average number of bonds across all molecules in the dataset.

question: Hey! Can you show me all the molecules that have many bonds? I need to know which ones are more complex.
sql:
WITH BondCountCTE AS (
    SELECT m.molecule_id, COUNT(b.bond_id) AS num_bonds,
           NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(b.bond_id)) AS quartile
    FROM molecule m
    LEFT JOIN bond b ON m.molecule_id = b.molecule_id
    GROUP BY m.molecule_id
)
SELECT molecule_id
FROM BondCountCTE
WHERE quartile = 4;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many bonds,' I mean molecules in the top 25% when ranking by total bonding interactions per molecule.

question: Show complex molecules.
sql: SELECT m.molecule_id FROM atom a INNER JOIN molecule m ON a.molecule_id = m.molecule_id GROUP BY m.molecule_id HAVING COUNT(a.atom_id) > 5;
hidden_knowledge: By complex molecules I mean those composed of more than five individual atoms.

question: Which molecules have many atoms connected to them?
sql: SELECT m.molecule_id, COUNT(a.atom_id) AS atom_count FROM molecule m INNER JOIN atom a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id GROUP BY m.molecule_id HAVING COUNT(a.atom_id) >= 3;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many atoms', I mean molecules with three or more constituent atoms.

question: I'm analyzing molecular structures for a chemistry report. I need to identify complex molecules to study their stability. Could you help me find them in the database?
sql:
SELECT m.molecule_id, COUNT(a.atom_id) AS atom_count
FROM molecule m
INNER JOIN atom a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id
GROUP BY m.molecule_id
HAVING COUNT(a.atom_id) > 5;
hidden_knowledge: By complex molecules, I mean those containing more than five constituent atoms in their structure.

question: List molecules with many atoms.
sql:
SELECT m.molecule_id 
FROM molecule m 
JOIN atom a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id 
GROUP BY m.molecule_id 
HAVING COUNT(a.atom_id) > 4;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many atoms', I mean molecules with more than four atoms.

question: List molecules with many atoms.
sql:
SELECT m.molecule_id 
FROM molecule m 
JOIN atom a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id 
GROUP BY m.molecule_id 
HAVING COUNT(a.atom_id) > (SELECT AVG(atom_count) FROM (SELECT COUNT(atom_id) AS atom_count FROM atom GROUP BY molecule_id) AS avg_table);
hidden_knowledge: By 'many atoms', I mean molecules with an atom count above the average number of atoms across all molecules.

question: Hey! Can you show me some high-cost Magic cards? I'm trying to figure out which ones are worth adding to my collection.
sql: SELECT name FROM cards WHERE convertedManaCost > 5;
hidden_knowledge: 'High-cost' refers to any card requiring more than 5 total mana to cast.

question: Hey! Can you show me some really high-mana-cost cards? I want to focus on those big spells for my game.
sql: WITH RankedCards AS (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY convertedManaCost DESC) AS rnk FROM cards), TotalCount AS (SELECT COUNT(*) AS total FROM cards) SELECT rc.* FROM RankedCards rc JOIN TotalCount tc WHERE rc.rnk <= tc.total * 0.1;
hidden_knowledge: By high-mana-cost I mean the very highest—top 10% of all Magic: The Gathering cards by their converted mana cost.

question: Show cards with high converted mana cost.
sql: WITH CTE AS (SELECT *, NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY convertedManaCost DESC) AS ntile FROM cards) SELECT * FROM CTE WHERE ntile = 1;
hidden_knowledge: By high converted mana cost, I mean cards in the top 10% of all cards when sorted by descending converted mana cost.

question: Which cards have a high EDHREC rank?
sql: SELECT * FROM cards WHERE edhrecRank < 500;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high EDHREC rank', I mean cards ranked among the top performers in EDH formats (lower numerical values indicate higher popularity).

question: Which Magic: The Gathering cards are considered popular?
sql:
SELECT name
FROM (
    SELECT name,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY edhrecRank ASC) AS edh_rank
    FROM cards
)
WHERE edh_rank <= 10;
hidden_knowledge: By popular, I mean cards with the highest rankings on EDHREC for Commander decks.

question: I'm designing an aggressive deck strategy for a limited game format where quick board control is key. I need to find all Magic: The Gathering cards with low mana costs that can be played early in the game to establish dominance.
sql: SELECT name, convertedManaCost FROM cards WHERE convertedManaCost <= 2 ORDER BY convertedManaCost ASC;
hidden_knowledge: By low mana cost I specifically mean one-drop or two-drop spells/creatures playable by turn 1-2 (costing ≤2 total mana).

question: I'm building a Commander deck and want to include highly ranked cards that are known staples in the format and are currently legal.
sql:
SELECT c.* 
FROM cards c
INNER JOIN legalities l ON c.uuid = l.uuid
WHERE l.format = 'commander' AND l.status = 'Legal'
AND c.edhrecRank IS NOT NULL
AND c.edhrecRank <= 2500
ORDER BY c.edhrecRank ASC;
hidden_knowledge: By highly ranked, I mean cards with an EDHREC rank within the top 2% of all Commander-relevant cards (lower EDHREC ranks indicate higher popularity).

question: Identify all transactions involving a high fuel amount.
sql: SELECT * FROM transactions_1k WHERE Amount > 30;
hidden_knowledge: By high fuel amount, I mean transactions exceeding 30 liters.

question: Identify customers with high fuel consumption.
sql: SELECT t.CustomerID FROM transactions_1k t GROUP BY t.CustomerID HAVING SUM(t.Amount * t.Price) > 5000;
hidden_knowledge: By high fuel consumption, I mean customers whose total spending on fuel exceeds $5000.

question: Identify customers with high fuel consumption based on their annual usage records.
sql:
WITH TotalConsumptionCTE AS (
    SELECT CustomerID, SUM(Consumption) AS TotalConsumption
    FROM yearmonth
    GROUP BY CustomerID
),
RankedCTE AS (
    SELECT *, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY TotalConsumption DESC) AS rnk,
           COUNT(*) OVER () AS total_customers
    FROM TotalConsumptionCTE
)
SELECT CustomerID
FROM RankedCTE
WHERE rnk <= CEIL(total_customers * 0.1);
hidden_knowledge: High fuel consumption refers to customers whose annual usage ranks in the top 10% compared to all customers.

question: Identify customers with high annual consumption levels.
sql: WITH AnnualCustomerConsumption AS (SELECT CustomerID, SUBSTR(Date, 1, 4) AS Year, SUM(Consumption) AS AnnualConsumption FROM yearmonth GROUP BY CustomerID, Year), CustomerTotalConsumption AS (SELECT CustomerID, SUM(AnnualConsumption) AS TotalAnnualSum FROM AnnualCustomerConsumption GROUP BY CustomerID), PercentileRanks AS (SELECT ctc.CustomerID, ctc.TotalAnnualSum, PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ctc.TotalAnnualSum DESC) AS RankPercentile FROM CustomerTotalConsumption ctc) SELECT pr.CustomerID FROM PercentileRanks pr WHERE pr.RankPercentile < 0.2;
hidden_knowledge: By high annual consumption levels I mean those who rank within the top 20% of all customers based on cumulative annual totals across multiple years.

question: Hey! Can you show me all customers who had high monthly fuel consumption? I just need their IDs and segments.
sql:
SELECT DISTINCT c.CustomerID, c.Segment
FROM customers c
JOIN yearmonth y ON c.CustomerID = y.CustomerID
WHERE y.Consumption > (
    SELECT AVG(Consumption)
    FROM yearmonth
);
hidden_knowledge: "High" means values exceeding the average monthly fuel consumption across all customers.

question: Hey! Can you show me all customers who had high monthly fuel consumption? I just need their IDs and segments.
sql:
SELECT DISTINCT c.CustomerID, c.Segment
FROM customers c
JOIN yearmonth y ON c.CustomerID = y.CustomerID
WHERE y.Consumption > 15;
-- Assuming "high" means more than 15 units of currency
hidden_knowledge: "High" means values above 15 units of currency in monthly fuel consumption.

question: Hey! Can you show me the customers with high fuel consumption? I want to target them for a special offer.
sql: WITH CustomerCons AS (SELECT CustomerID, AVG(Consumption) AS AvgCons FROM yearmonth GROUP BY CustomerID), PercentileCTE AS (SELECT CustomerID, AvgCons, PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY AvgCons) AS pr FROM CustomerCons) SELECT c.CustomerID FROM PercentileCTE c WHERE c.pr >= 0.8;
hidden_knowledge: By high fuel consumption, I mean customers in the top 20% of average monthly consumption compared to all customers.

question: Show large fuel purchases made in August 2012.
sql: SELECT * FROM transactions_1k WHERE ProductID = 2 AND Amount > 40 AND Date >= '2012-08-01' AND Date < '2012-09-01';
hidden_knowledge: By large fuel purchases, I mean transactions where more than 40 liters were purchased.

question: Show customers with high monthly consumption.
sql: SELECT y.CustomerID FROM yearmonth y GROUP BY y.CustomerID HAVING AVG(y.Consumption) > (SELECT AVG(Consumption) FROM yearmonth)
hidden_knowledge: By high monthly consumption, I mean customers whose average monthly fuel usage exceeds the overall average fuel usage across all customers.

question: Show customers with high fuel consumption.
sql: WITH CustomerAvg AS (SELECT CustomerID, AVG(Consumption) AS AvgCons FROM yearmonth GROUP BY CustomerID), OverallAvg AS (SELECT AVG(AvgCons) AS OverallAvg FROM CustomerAvg) SELECT ca.CustomerID FROM CustomerAvg ca WHERE ca.AvgCons > (SELECT OverallAvg FROM OverallAvg);
hidden_knowledge: By high fuel consumption, I mean customers whose average monthly consumption exceeds the overall average monthly consumption across all customers.

question: Show customers with high fuel consumption.
sql: WITH CustomerAvg AS (SELECT CustomerID, AVG(Consumption) AS AvgCons FROM yearmonth GROUP BY CustomerID), RankedCustomers AS (SELECT CustomerID, AvgCons, NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY AvgCons DESC) AS Decile FROM CustomerAvg) SELECT CustomerID FROM RankedCustomers WHERE Decile = 1;
hidden_knowledge: By high fuel consumption, I mean customers who fall within the top 10% of consumers based on their average monthly fuel consumption.

question: Which customers have made many transactions?
sql:
SELECT c.CustomerID 
FROM customers c 
INNER JOIN transactions_1k t ON c.CustomerID = t.CustomerID 
GROUP BY c.CustomerID 
HAVING COUNT(t.TransactionID) > (
    SELECT AVG(transaction_count) * 2 
    FROM (
        SELECT COUNT(TransactionID) AS transaction_count 
        FROM transactions_1k 
        GROUP BY CustomerID
    )
);
hidden_knowledge: By 'many' I mean customers whose transaction frequency exceeds twice the average customer transaction count.

question: Which customers have high fuel consumption based on their monthly records?
sql: WITH ConsumptionRankCTE AS (SELECT CustomerID, SUM(Consumption) AS TotalConsumption, NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY SUM(Consumption) DESC) AS ConsumptionDecile FROM yearmonth GROUP BY CustomerID) SELECT r.CustomerID, r.TotalConsumption, c.Segment, c.Currency FROM ConsumptionRankCTE r JOIN customers c ON r.CustomerID = c.CustomerID WHERE r.ConsumptionDecile = 1 ORDER BY r.TotalConsumption DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high fuel consumption', I mean customers in the top 10% of total consumption when ranked against all customers' cumulative monthly consumption.

question: Our marketing team is planning a loyalty program targeting customers with high monthly fuel consumption to increase retention. We need to identify these top consumers based on their historical monthly consumption data.
sql: SELECT CustomerID FROM yearmonth WHERE Consumption > 1500;
hidden_knowledge: By high monthly fuel consumption, I mean customers who consistently consume more than 1500 liters per month.

question: Our marketing team wants to target our most active customers for a loyalty program. Could you identify customers who have high fuel consumption in the last quarter?
sql:
SELECT c.CustomerID, SUM(y.Consumption) AS total_consumption
FROM customers c
JOIN yearmonth y ON c.CustomerID = y.CustomerID
WHERE y.Date BETWEEN '201207' AND '201209'
GROUP BY c.CustomerID
HAVING SUM(y.Consumption) > 1500;
hidden_knowledge: By high fuel consumption, I mean any customer whose total fuel usage in the last quarter exceeds 1,500 liters.

question: We are launching a targeted marketing campaign for our most valuable customers. Could you identify customers who have high fuel consumption based on their historical data so we can offer them premium loyalty benefits?
sql: WITH AvgConsumptionCTE AS (SELECT AVG(Consumption) AS AvgCons FROM yearmonth) SELECT y.CustomerID, SUM(y.Consumption) AS TotalCons FROM yearmonth y JOIN AvgConsumptionCTE ON y.Consumption > AvgConsumptionCTE.AvgCons GROUP BY y.CustomerID ORDER BY TotalCons DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high fuel consumption', we mean customers whose monthly consumption exceeds the average monthly consumption across all customers.

question: Our marketing team wants to target customers with high fuel consumption for a loyalty rewards campaign. Could you identify customers who have shown significant consumption patterns that might qualify them for premium tier benefits? We're interested in understanding both absolute high-volume consumers and those who recently experienced a notable increase in usage compared to their historical behavior.
sql:
WITH HistoricalAverages AS (
    SELECT
        CustomerID,
        AVG(Consumption) AS AvgMonthlyConsumption,
        MAX(Date) AS LatestMonth
    FROM
        yearmonth
    GROUP BY
        CustomerID
),
RecentActivity AS (
    SELECT
        ym.CustomerID,
        ym.Consumption AS RecentConsumption,
        ha.AvgMonthlyConsumption,
        ha.LatestMonth,
        CASE 
            WHEN ym.Consumption > 2 * ha.AvgMonthlyConsumption THEN 1 
            ELSE 0 
        END AS SpikeFlag
    FROM
        yearmonth ym
    INNER JOIN HistoricalAverages ha 
        ON ym.CustomerID = ha.CustomerID
    WHERE
        ym.Date = ha.LatestMonth
)
SELECT
    r.CustomerID,
    c.Segment,
    c.Currency,
    r.RecentConsumption,
    r.AvgMonthlyConsumption,
    r.RecentConsumption / r.AvgMonthlyConsumption AS ConsumptionRatio
FROM
    RecentActivity r
INNER JOIN customers c ON r.CustomerID = c.CustomerID
WHERE
    r.SpikeFlag = 1;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high consumption', we're referring to customers whose most recent month's fuel usage exceeds twice their historical monthly average - indicating a sudden surge in usage patterns.

question: Customers with high consumption.
sql: SELECT y.CustomerID FROM yearmonth y GROUP BY y.CustomerID HAVING SUM(y.Consumption) IN (SELECT SUM(Consumption) FROM yearmonth GROUP BY CustomerID ORDER BY SUM(Consumption) DESC LIMIT 5);
hidden_knowledge: By high consumption, I mean the top 5 customers with the highest total annual consumption.

question: Customers with high consumption.
sql: SELECT y.CustomerID FROM yearmonth y GROUP BY y.CustomerID HAVING SUM(y.Consumption) > 1500;
hidden_knowledge: By high consumption, I mean customers whose total annual consumption exceeds 1500 units.

question: Customers with high consumption.
sql:
WITH Aggregated AS (
    SELECT 
        y.CustomerID,
        SUM(y.Consumption) AS TotalConsumption,
        RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(y.Consumption) DESC) AS rnk,
        COUNT(*) OVER () AS total_customers
    FROM yearmonth y
    GROUP BY y.CustomerID
),
CutoffRank AS (
    SELECT ROUND(total_customers * 0.1) AS cutoff_rank
    FROM Aggregated
    LIMIT 1
)
SELECT DISTINCT y.CustomerID
FROM Aggregated y
CROSS JOIN CutoffRank c
WHERE y.rnk <= c.cutoff_rank;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high consumption', I mean customers in the top 10% of total consumption across all months.

question: Retrieve the identities of superheroes who possess notably high intelligence attributes.
sql: WITH IntelligenceRank AS ( SELECT s.superhero_name, s.full_name, ha.attribute_value, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ha.attribute_value DESC) AS intelligence_rank FROM superhero s JOIN hero_attribute ha ON s.id = ha.hero_id JOIN attribute a ON ha.attribute_id = a.id WHERE a.attribute_name = 'Intelligence' ) SELECT superhero_name, full_name FROM IntelligenceRank WHERE intelligence_rank <= 10;
hidden_knowledge: By 'notably high intelligence', I mean the top ten superheroes ranked by their intelligence attribute values.

question: Alright mate, can you list the good alignment heroes that have strong attributes? Just wondering who's out there.
sql: WITH strong_attributes AS ( SELECT hero_id FROM hero_attribute WHERE attribute_value > 90 ) SELECT DISTINCT s.superhero_name FROM superhero s JOIN alignment a ON s.alignment_id = a.id JOIN strong_attributes sa ON s.id = sa.hero_id WHERE a.alignment = 'Good';
hidden_knowledge: By strong attributes, I mean any attribute value above 90.

question: Display superheroes with notable strength attributes
sql: WITH StrengthRanking AS ( SELECT hero_id, attribute_value, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY attribute_value DESC) AS strength_rank FROM hero_attribute WHERE attribute_id = 2 ) SELECT s.superhero_name, s.full_name, ha.attribute_value AS strength FROM superhero s JOIN hero_attribute ha ON s.id = ha.hero_id JOIN StrengthRanking sr ON ha.hero_id = sr.hero_id AND ha.attribute_value = sr.attribute_value WHERE sr.strength_rank <= 10;

question: Find the popular publishers and the number of heroes they have created.
sql: SELECT p.publisher_name, COUNT(s.superhero_name) AS number_of_superheroes FROM publisher p LEFT JOIN superhero s ON p.id = s.publisher_id GROUP BY p.publisher_name HAVING COUNT(s.superhero_name) > 10;
hidden_knowledge: By popular publishers, I mean those with more than ten superheroes.

question: I'm conducting research on superhero demographics and need to analyze the distribution of alignment types among superheroes. Could you provide me with information about superheroes who have a predominantly positive alignment? This will help me understand the prevalence of heroic figures in our dataset.
sql: SELECT s.superhero_name, s.full_name, a.alignment FROM superhero s JOIN alignment a ON s.alignment_id = a.id WHERE a.alignment IN ('Good', 'Neutral');
hidden_knowledge: By predominantly positive alignment, I mean superheroes who are either Good or Neutral.

question: Heavy superheroes with notable powers.
sql: WITH PowerfulHeroes AS ( SELECT hero_id, COUNT(*) AS power_count FROM hero_power GROUP BY hero_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 5 ) SELECT s.superhero_name, s.weight_kg, p.power_name FROM superhero s JOIN hero_power hp ON s.id = hp.hero_id JOIN superpower p ON hp.power_id = p.id JOIN PowerfulHeroes ph ON s.id = ph.hero_id WHERE s.weight_kg > 90;
hidden_knowledge: By heavy superheroes with notable powers, I mean those weighing over 90 kg who possess more than five powers.

question: Yo! Can you show me some molecules that have fairly complex bonding? I'm curious about their structures.
sql: SELECT m.molecule_id, m.label, COUNT(b.bond_id) AS total_bonds FROM molecule m INNER JOIN bond b ON m.molecule_id = b.molecule_id GROUP BY m.molecule_id, m.label HAVING COUNT(b.bond_id) > 5;
hidden_knowledge: By 'fairly complex bonding' I mean molecules with more than five bonds total.

question: Hey there! I need to see some complex molecules with a lot of atoms. Can you help me find those?
sql: WITH atom_counts AS (SELECT molecule_id, COUNT(atom_id) AS atom_count FROM atom GROUP BY molecule_id), bond_counts AS (SELECT molecule_id, COUNT(bond_id) AS bond_count FROM bond GROUP BY molecule_id) SELECT m.molecule_id, m.label, ac.atom_count FROM molecule m JOIN atom_counts ac ON m.molecule_id = ac.molecule_id JOIN bond_counts bc ON m.molecule_id = bc.molecule_id WHERE ac.atom_count > 10 AND bc.bond_count > 15;
hidden_knowledge: By 'a lot' I mean more than 10 atoms and more than 15 bonds in the molecule.

question: Find molecules with many atoms
sql: SELECT m.molecule_id, m.label FROM molecule m JOIN atom a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id GROUP BY m.molecule_id, m.label HAVING COUNT(a.atom_id) > 10;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many atoms', I mean molecules that have more than 10 atoms.

question: Which molecules contain many atoms of the same element?
sql: SELECT a.molecule_id, COUNT(*) as atom_count, a.element FROM atom a GROUP BY a.molecule_id, a.element HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many', I mean more than 5 atoms of the same element in a molecule.

question: Which molecules have many atoms?
sql: WITH AtomCounts AS (SELECT molecule_id, COUNT(atom_id) AS atom_count FROM atom GROUP BY molecule_id) SELECT m.molecule_id, m.label FROM molecule m JOIN AtomCounts a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id WHERE a.atom_count > (SELECT AVG(atom_count) * 1.5 FROM AtomCounts);
hidden_knowledge: By many, I mean molecules with an above-average number of atoms, specifically more than 1.5 times the average.

question: I'm conducting research on molecular structures and need to identify molecules that contain a significant number of chlorine atoms. This will help me understand the prevalence of chlorine in various chemical compounds. The results will be used to assess the environmental impact of these molecules.
sql:
WITH ChlorineCount AS (
    SELECT
        a.molecule_id,
        COUNT(*) AS chlorine_count
    FROM
        atom a
    WHERE
        a.element = 'cl'
    GROUP BY
        a.molecule_id
)
SELECT
    m.molecule_id,
    m.label,
    cc.chlorine_count
FROM
    molecule m
JOIN
    ChlorineCount cc ON m.molecule_id = cc.molecule_id
WHERE
    cc.chlorine_count > 3;
hidden_knowledge: By a significant number of chlorine atoms, I mean molecules that have more than three chlorine atoms.

question: Popular elements.
sql: SELECT a.element, COUNT(*) AS element_count FROM atom a GROUP BY a.element ORDER BY element_count DESC LIMIT 3;
hidden_knowledge: By popular elements, I mean the three most frequently occurring elements.

question: Popular elements.
sql: SELECT a.element, COUNT(*) AS element_count FROM atom a GROUP BY a.element HAVING COUNT(*) > 5 ORDER BY element_count DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By popular elements, I mean those appearing more than 5 times in the database.

question: List all cards that have high foiling rates.
sql: SELECT name FROM cards WHERE hasFoil = 1 AND hasNonFoil = 1 AND isPromo = 1;
hidden_knowledge: By high foiling rates, I mean the card is both available in foil and non-foil versions and it is a promo card.

question: List all cards that have high foiling rates.

question: Can you find me some common cards that are still pretty good? I'm working with a limited budget.
sql: SELECT name, rarity FROM cards WHERE rarity = 'common' AND edhrecRank < 500;
hidden_knowledge: By pretty good, I mean common cards that rank below 500 on EDHREC.

question: Hey, can you show me some popular Magic cards? I'm looking to build a new deck.
sql: SELECT c.name, COUNT(col.user_id) AS collection_count FROM cards c LEFT JOIN collections col ON c.uuid = col.card_uuid GROUP BY c.uuid ORDER BY collection_count DESC LIMIT 10;

question: Hey, can you show me some popular Magic cards? I'm looking to build a new deck.
sql: WITH avg_rank AS (SELECT AVG(edhrecRank) as avg_rank FROM cards WHERE edhrecRank IS NOT NULL) SELECT * FROM cards, avg_rank WHERE cards.edhrecRank < avg_rank.avg_rank AND cards.edhrecRank IS NOT NULL ORDER BY cards.edhrecRank ASC;
hidden_knowledge: By popular, I mean cards that are more played than the average card in the database.

question: List common and uncommon cards with high rarity value
sql: SELECT id, name, rarity FROM cards WHERE rarity IN ('common', 'uncommon') AND (rarity = 'uncommon' OR (rarity = 'common' AND hasFoil = 1)) ORDER BY rarity DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high rarity value' I mean uncommon cards or common cards that have a foil version.

question: List common and uncommon cards with high rarity value
sql: SELECT id, name, rarity FROM cards WHERE rarity IN ('common', 'uncommon') AND (rarity = 'uncommon' OR (rarity = 'common' AND hasNonFoil = 1)) ORDER BY rarity DESC;

question: I'm curating a collection of Magic: The Gathering cards for a beginner's guide and need to include 'popular' cards that are frequently used in basic decks. These cards should help new players learn the fundamentals of the game.
sql: SELECT name, types, rarity FROM cards WHERE rarity = 'common' ORDER BY RANDOM() LIMIT 20;

question: Show me popular cards.
sql: SELECT name FROM cards WHERE edhrecRank < 1000 ORDER BY edhrecRank;
hidden_knowledge: By popular, I mean cards with an EDHREC rank below 1000.

question: Retrieve the transaction records for customers with high consumption levels.
sql: WITH HighConsumptionCustomers AS ( SELECT CustomerID FROM yearmonth WHERE Consumption > (SELECT AVG(Consumption) * 1.5 FROM yearmonth) ) SELECT t.TransactionID, t.Date, t.Time, t.CustomerID, t.GasStationID, t.ProductID, t.Amount, t.Price FROM transactions_1k t INNER JOIN HighConsumptionCustomers h ON t.CustomerID = h.CustomerID;
hidden_knowledge: By high consumption levels, I mean customers whose consumption is more than 50% above the average.

question: Can you find me some big spenders? I want to see which customers spend the most at our gas stations.
sql: SELECT c.CustomerID, c.Segment, COUNT(t.TransactionID) AS transaction_count FROM customers c INNER JOIN transactions_1k t ON c.CustomerID = t.CustomerID GROUP BY c.CustomerID, c.Segment ORDER BY transaction_count DESC LIMIT 10;
hidden_knowledge: By big spenders, I mean customers who have made the most transactions.

question: Can you find me some big spenders? I want to see which customers spend the most at our gas stations.
sql: SELECT c.CustomerID, c.Segment, SUM(t.Amount) AS total_spent FROM customers c INNER JOIN transactions_1k t ON c.CustomerID = t.CustomerID GROUP BY c.CustomerID, c.Segment ORDER BY total_spent DESC LIMIT 10;
hidden_knowledge: By big spenders, I mean customers who have spent the most money in total.

question: Hey, can you show me the gas stations that have a lot of transactions? I'm trying to figure out which ones are super busy.
sql: SELECT gs.GasStationID, gs.ChainID, gs.Country, gs.Segment, COUNT(t.TransactionID) as transaction_count FROM gasstations gs JOIN transactions_1k t ON gs.GasStationID = t.GasStationID GROUP BY gs.GasStationID, gs.ChainID, gs.Country, gs.Segment HAVING COUNT(t.TransactionID) > (SELECT AVG(transaction_count) FROM (SELECT COUNT(TransactionID) as transaction_count FROM transactions_1k GROUP BY GasStationID) subq)
hidden_knowledge: By 'a lot of transactions,' I mean more than the average number of transactions per gas station.

question: Hey, can you show me the gas stations that have a lot of transactions? I'm trying to figure out which ones are super busy.
sql: WITH StationStats AS (SELECT GasStationID, COUNT(TransactionID) as transaction_count, PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(TransactionID) DESC) as percentile FROM transactions_1k GROUP BY GasStationID), TopStations AS (SELECT GasStationID FROM StationStats WHERE percentile < 0.1) SELECT gs.GasStationID, gs.ChainID, gs.Country, gs.Segment FROM gasstations gs JOIN TopStations ts ON gs.GasStationID = ts.GasStationID
hidden_knowledge: By 'a lot of transactions,' I mean in the top 10% of gas stations by transaction volume.

question: Show gas stations with many transactions
sql: SELECT g.GasStationID, g.Country, g.Segment, COUNT(t.TransactionID) AS transaction_count FROM gasstations g LEFT JOIN transactions_1k t ON g.GasStationID = t.GasStationID GROUP BY g.GasStationID, g.Country, g.Segment HAVING COUNT(t.TransactionID) > (SELECT AVG(transaction_count) * 2 FROM (SELECT COUNT(TransactionID) AS transaction_count FROM transactions_1k GROUP BY GasStationID) AS subquery)
hidden_knowledge: By 'many transactions', I mean gas stations that have more than twice the average number of transactions per station.

question: Display customers with significant consumption variations between consecutive months
sql:
WITH MonthlyConsumption AS (
    SELECT
        CustomerID,
        Date,
        Consumption,
        LAG(Consumption) OVER (PARTITION BY CustomerID ORDER BY CAST(Date AS INTEGER)) AS PreviousMonthConsumption
    FROM yearmonth
),
ConsumptionVariations AS (
    SELECT
        CustomerID,
        Date,
        Consumption,
        PreviousMonthConsumption,
        (Consumption - PreviousMonthConsumption) AS Variation,
        ABS(Consumption - PreviousMonthConsumption) AS AbsoluteVariation
    FROM MonthlyConsumption
    WHERE PreviousMonthConsumption IS NOT NULL
),
SignificantVariations AS (
    SELECT
        cv.*
    FROM ConsumptionVariations cv
    WHERE cv.AbsoluteVariation > (
        SELECT AVG(ABS(c.AbsoluteVariation)) * 2
        FROM ConsumptionVariations c
    )
)
SELECT
    c.CustomerID,
    cu.Segment,
    sv.Date AS Month,
    sv.Consumption AS CurrentMonthConsumption,
    sv.PreviousMonthConsumption,
    sv.Variation,
    sv.AbsoluteVariation
FROM SignificantVariations sv
JOIN customers c ON sv.CustomerID = c.CustomerID
JOIN customers cu ON sv.CustomerID = cu.CustomerID
ORDER BY sv.AbsoluteVariation DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By significant consumption variations, I mean variations that exceed twice the average variation across all customers.

question: Display customers with significant consumption variations between consecutive months
sql:
WITH MonthlyConsumption AS (
    SELECT
        CustomerID,
        Date,
        Consumption,
        LAG(Consumption) OVER (PARTITION BY CustomerID ORDER BY CAST(Date AS INTEGER)) AS PreviousMonthConsumption
    FROM yearmonth
),
ConsumptionVariations AS (
    SELECT
        CustomerID,
        Date,
        Consumption,
        PreviousMonthConsumption,
        (Consumption - PreviousMonthConsumption) AS Variation,
        ABS(Consumption - PreviousMonthConsumption) AS AbsoluteVariation,
        (ABS(Consumption - PreviousMonthConsumption) / NULLIF(PreviousMonthConsumption, 0)) * 100 AS PercentageVariation
    FROM MonthlyConsumption
    WHERE PreviousMonthConsumption IS NOT NULL
),
SignificantVariations AS (
    SELECT
        cv.*
    FROM ConsumptionVariations cv
    WHERE cv.PercentageVariation > 50 OR cv.PercentageVariation < -50
)
SELECT
    c.CustomerID,
    cu.Segment,
    sv.Date AS Month,
    sv.Consumption AS CurrentMonthConsumption,
    sv.PreviousMonthConsumption,
    sv.Variation,
    sv.AbsoluteVariation,
    sv.PercentageVariation
FROM SignificantVariations sv
JOIN customers c ON sv.CustomerID = c.CustomerID
JOIN customers cu ON sv.CustomerID = cu.CustomerID
ORDER BY ABS(sv.PercentageVariation) DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By significant consumption variations, I mean variations that are greater than 50% increase or decrease compared to the previous month.

question: Can you show me the old gas stations in the database?
sql: SELECT GasStationID FROM gasstations WHERE GasStationID < 45;
hidden_knowledge: By old gas stations, I mean those with IDs less than 45.

question: Which gas stations are located in countries with expensive fuel prices?
sql: SELECT DISTINCT gs.GasStationID, gs.Country FROM gasstations gs JOIN transactions_1k t ON gs.GasStationID = t.GasStationID WHERE t.Price > 2.5 AND gs.Country IN ('CZE', 'GER', 'FRA');
hidden_knowledge: By expensive fuel prices, I mean prices above 2.5 units of currency in countries like CZE, GER, and FRA.

question: For a marketing campaign targeting high-value customers, I need to identify those who spend a considerable amount of money on fuel. This will allow us to offer them exclusive discounts and premium services.
sql: SELECT c.CustomerID, SUM(t.Amount * t.Price) AS total_spent FROM customers c INNER JOIN transactions_1k t ON c.CustomerID = t.CustomerID GROUP BY c.CustomerID HAVING SUM(t.Amount * t.Price) > 5000;
hidden_knowledge: By considerable amount of money, I mean total spending above 5000 units.

question: I'm analyzing customer consumption patterns for our gas station network. I need to identify our most valuable customers who have shown consistently high consumption over time. This information will help us target our premium loyalty program to the right audience.
sql: WITH CustomerConsumption AS (  SELECT CustomerID, AVG(Consumption) AS AvgConsumption,  PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY AVG(Consumption) DESC) AS ConsumptionRank  FROM yearmonth  GROUP BY CustomerID) SELECT c.CustomerID, c.Segment, cc.AvgConsumption FROM customers c  JOIN CustomerConsumption cc ON c.CustomerID = cc.CustomerID  WHERE cc.ConsumptionRank < 0.1;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high consumption', I mean customers in the top 10% of average monthly consumption across all our customers. We want to focus on the absolute highest spenders regardless of consistency.

question: I'm analyzing customer consumption patterns for our gas station network. I need to identify our most valuable customers who have shown consistently high consumption over time. This information will help us target our premium loyalty program to the right audience.
sql: WITH MonthlyConsumption AS (  SELECT CustomerID, Date, Consumption,  LAG(Consumption, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY CustomerID ORDER BY CAST(Date AS INTEGER)) AS PreviousMonthConsumption  FROM yearmonth), ConsistentCustomers AS (  SELECT CustomerID, COUNT(*) AS ConsistentMonths  FROM MonthlyConsumption  WHERE Consumption > PreviousMonthConsumption AND PreviousMonthConsumption IS NOT NULL  GROUP BY CustomerID  HAVING COUNT(*) >= 6) SELECT c.CustomerID, c.Segment, AVG(y.Consumption) AS AvgConsumption FROM customers c  JOIN ConsistentCustomers cc ON c.CustomerID = cc.CustomerID  JOIN yearmonth y ON c.CustomerID = y.CustomerID  GROUP BY c.CustomerID, c.Segment;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high consumption', I mean customers who have shown increasing consumption compared to the previous month for at least six consecutive months. We're looking for customers with upward trends rather than just high absolute values.

question: Customers with many transactions
sql: SELECT CustomerID FROM transactions_1k GROUP BY CustomerID HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
hidden_knowledge: By customers with many transactions, I mean those with more than 5 transactions.

question: Top fuel products
sql: WITH product_sales AS ( SELECT p.ProductID, p.Description, SUM(t.Amount) as total_amount FROM products p JOIN transactions_1k t ON p.ProductID = t.ProductID GROUP BY p.ProductID, p.Description ORDER BY total_amount DESC LIMIT 5 ), top_customers AS ( SELECT tp.ProductID, c.CustomerID, COUNT(*) as purchase_count FROM transactions_1k t JOIN product_sales tp ON t.ProductID = tp.ProductID JOIN customers c ON t.CustomerID = c.CustomerID GROUP BY tp.ProductID, c.CustomerID HAVING COUNT(*) > 5 ), customer_stats AS ( SELECT tc.ProductID, AVG(ym.Consumption) as avg_consumption FROM top_customers tc JOIN yearmonth ym ON tc.CustomerID = ym.CustomerID GROUP BY tc.ProductID ) SELECT ps.ProductID, ps.Description, ps.total_amount, cs.avg_consumption FROM product_sales ps JOIN customer_stats cs ON ps.ProductID = cs.ProductID;
hidden_knowledge: By top fuel products I mean those with the highest total amount sold.

question: Identify superheroes with a notably high power level.
sql: SELECT s.superhero_name FROM superhero s INNER JOIN hero_attribute ha ON s.id = ha.hero_id WHERE ha.attribute_id = (SELECT id FROM attribute WHERE attribute_name = 'Power') AND ha.attribute_value > 80;
hidden_knowledge: When I say 'high power level', I am referring to the 'Power' attribute score exceeding 80.

question: Hey, could you show me superheroes with a lot of powers? I'm just curious who's really stacked.
sql: SELECT superhero_name FROM superhero WHERE id IN (SELECT hero_id FROM hero_power GROUP BY hero_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 3);

question: Show me superheroes with many abilities.

question: Show me superheroes with many powers.

question: Which superheroes have a lot of power?

question: Which superheroes have a lot of power?

question: I'm researching superheroes for a project and I'm trying to identify those with a substantial physical presence. Could you list the superheroes who are notably strong?

hidden_knowledge: When I say 'notably strong', I'm looking for superheroes who have a high score in the 'Strength' attribute according to the hero attribute table.

question: Superheroes with many attributes.

question: Identify molecules with a large number of atoms.

question: Hey, could you show me molecules with a lot of atoms? I'm just trying to get an idea of the bigger ones.

question: Hey, could you show me the molecules with a lot of atoms? I'm just trying to get a feel for the bigger ones.
sql: SELECT m.molecule_id, m.label FROM molecule m JOIN atom a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id GROUP BY m.molecule_id HAVING COUNT(a.atom_id) > (SELECT AVG(atom_count) FROM (SELECT COUNT(atom_id) AS atom_count FROM atom GROUP BY molecule_id) AS AtomCounts);

question: Which molecules have many atoms?

question: Molecules with many atoms.

question: Identify customers with a high consumption of gas.

question: Our marketing team is analyzing customer spending habits. We're trying to identify customers who make relatively large transactions, as these are our key targets for loyalty programs. Could you provide a list of customers who have made a high number of purchases?

question: Our marketing team is analyzing customer spending habits. We're trying to identify customers who make relatively large transactions, as these are our key targets for loyalty programs. Could you provide a list of customers who have made a high number of purchases?

question: Our marketing team is analyzing customer spending habits. We're trying to identify customers who make a 'substantial' number of transactions. Could you provide a list of those customers, along with their total spending amount?
